questions.text: What is the zero value of a string type in Go?
options: nil
"null"
"" (empty string)
undefined
questions.explanation: In Go, every type has a zero value. For strings, it's the empty string "". Unlike some languages, Go strings cannot be nil — only pointer types, slices, maps, channels, interfaces, and functions can be nil.

questions.text: How does a type satisfy an interface in Go?
options: By explicitly declaring 'implements InterfaceName'
By inheriting from the interface type
By implementing all the methods declared in the interface
By registering the type with the interface at runtime
questions.correct: By implementing all the methods declared in the interface
questions.explanation: Go interfaces are satisfied implicitly. If a type has all the methods that an interface requires (with matching signatures), it automatically satisfies that interface. There's no 'implements' keyword — this is called structural typing.

questions.text: What is the key difference between a value receiver and a pointer receiver on a method?
options: Value receivers are faster because they avoid heap allocation
Pointer receivers allow the method to modify the original value, while value receivers work on a copy
Value receivers can only be used with basic types, not structs
There is no practical difference — it's just a style preference
questions.correct: Pointer receivers allow the method to modify the original value, while value receivers work on a copy
questions.explanation: A value receiver gets a copy of the value, so any modifications are lost when the method returns. A pointer receiver gets a reference to the original value and can modify it. Pointer receivers are also preferred for large structs to avoid copying overhead.

questions.text: In what order do deferred function calls execute?
options: First-in, first-out (FIFO) — same order as declared
Last-in, first-out (LIFO) — reverse order of declaration
In parallel — all at the same time
Random order — no guaranteed ordering
questions.correct: Last-in, first-out (LIFO) — reverse order of declaration
questions.explanation: Deferred calls are pushed onto a stack. When the enclosing function returns, they execute in LIFO order — the last defer registered runs first. This ensures proper cleanup ordering (e.g., unlock a mutex before closing a file that was opened first).

questions.text: What does the `...` operator do in `func sum(nums ...int) int`?
options: It makes the function accept exactly three int arguments
It makes nums a pointer to an int array
It makes the function variadic — accepting zero or more int arguments as a slice
It spreads a single int into multiple values
questions.correct: It makes the function variadic — accepting zero or more int arguments as a slice
questions.explanation: The ...int syntax makes 'nums' a variadic parameter. Inside the function, nums is a []int slice. The function can be called with any number of int arguments: sum(), sum(1), sum(1,2,3). You can also pass a slice with sum(slice...).
